draft.introduction: The Sāmaññaphala Sutta records the Buddha’s conversation with King Ajātasattu. It surveys rival philosophies before describing the progressive fruits of monastic practice.

translation.text: King Ajātasattu approached the Blessed One and asked: “Lord, the craftsmen live off the fruits of their crafts, visible here and now. Can the Lord point out a similar fruit of the contemplative life, visible here and now?”
translation.original_text: rājā māgadho ajātasattu vedehiputto bhagavantaṃ etad avoca

translation.text: With his mind thus concentrated, purified, cleansed, the monk directs it toward the knowledge of the destruction of the mental fermentations. He discerns: “This is suffering. This is the origin of suffering. This is the cessation of suffering. This is the way leading to the cessation of suffering.” His mind is released.
translation.original_text: so evaṃ samāhite citte parisuddhe āsavakhāyaṇaṃ ñāṇaya cittaṃ abhininnamesati

translator_notes.note: The sutta’s survey of six rival teachers is the richest surviving description of non-Buddhist Indian philosophy from this period, preserving doctrines of determinism, materialism, skepticism, and atomism that would otherwise be lost.
